hook: Steel-cutting alleys where artists moved in when the foundries emptied out
facts: Peaked in the 1970s-80s with over 1,700 small metal workshops and iron foundries
Artists from Hongdae and Daehangno began arriving around 2005 for cheap rent
Officially named Mullae Art Village in 2013 to protect the neighbourhood
Still has working ironworks operating alongside studios
legend: Locals say the day belongs to the factory workers and the night to the artists
w: Once most of the country's steel passed through these lanes, screaming through 1,700-odd foundries. Then the factories fled overseas and painters crept into the oil-stained sheds for the rent. Now sparks fly by day, studio lights by night, the two trades sharing the same grime. Rust as a medium.

hook: Korea's foremost shamanist shrine, exiled uphill by the Japanese
facts: Origins date to 1395, when it sat atop Namsan to honour the mountain spirit Mongmyeok
Moved to the Inwangsan ridge in 1925 after the Japanese built a Shinto shrine on the old site
Designated National Folklore Cultural Heritage No. 28 for its 21 shaman paintings
Female mudang still perform gut rites here for fortune, healing and marriage
legend: They say spirits invoked here can bless a marriage, bring luck or cure illness
w: Founded in 1395 atop Namsan to feed the mountain spirit, this little shrine got hauled up Inwangsan in 1925 when the colonial regime planted a Shinto shrine on its ground. Inside hang 21 shaman paintings. Out front, female mudang still drum and dance the gut, summoning spirits to mend a marriage or a body. Korea's old religion, unhushed.

hook: Weathered crags shaped like robed monks, sacred to shamans and would-be mothers
facts: Stand on the western ridge of Inwangsan just above Guksadang shrine
Named Seoul City Folklore Material No. 4 for their ritual significance
Pitted surfaces resemble monks absorbed in Zen meditation
Long a site of shamanist prayer and a subject of Joseon-era poetry
legend: Women have long prayed at the rocks for a son; Joseon poets puzzled over their robed shapes
w: Two pocked boulders on the granite ridge, looking for all the world like a pair of monks hunched in meditation, robes and all. Joseon poets wrote them into verse; shamans still climb here to pray, and women have long come asking for a son. Eleven small shrines line the path up. Stone that absorbs other people's wishes.

hook: Seoul's oldest bookshop, a 1951 hanok now half bookshop, half cafe
facts: Opened in 1951 by a young couple, Cho Dae-sik and Kwon Oh-nam, in Seochon
The founding couple's hanok living quarters run into the back of the shop
Declared a municipal heritage site, blue door and weathered sign preserved
Now also operates as a cafe and small gallery around the courtyard
w: A young couple opened this shop in 1951, back when books were scarce enough to trade like currency, and lived in the hanok rooms that still bleed into the shelving at the rear. The blue door, the faded sign, the stacks of foxed paperbacks all stayed when the city listed it. Order a coffee among 70-odd years of other people's reading.

hook: A hillside cafe on the Bukaksan slopes that played a house in Coffee Prince
facts: Name means cafe at the corner of the mountain
Perched on the slopes of Bukaksan in the quiet Buam-dong neighbourhood
Served as a lead character's home in the 2007 drama Coffee Prince
Terraces look out over the rooftops and fortress wall of northern Seoul
w: The name translates as the corner of the mountain, which sells it short. Clinging to the Bukaksan slope above sleepy Buam-dong, this two-storey cafe stood in for a character's home in Coffee Prince back in 2007 and never quite shook the pilgrims. Skip the lore, take the terrace: rooftops and old fortress wall, all the way down.

hook: A novelist's 1930s hanok serving jujube tea, run by his granddaughter
facts: Former home of writer Lee Tae-jun, who lived here from 1933 to 1946
Setting for several of his stories and his 1943 novel Haebang Jeonhu
The name means a house where literati gather in the mountains
Operated as a teahouse since 1998 by the writer's granddaughter
w: Lee Tae-jun wrote here from 1933 until he vanished north in the late 1940s, and several of his stories take place in these very rooms. His granddaughter now pours daechucha and ssanghwacha across the same wooden floors, the garden framed in the doorways. The name promises a mountain house where writers gather. It mostly still does.

hook: A 1941 market where you buy lunch with brass coins and build your own tray
facts: Established in 1941 to serve Japanese residents during the colonial period
Grew with Korean vendors after the Korean War as Seoul's population swelled
The yeopjeon brass-coin lunchbox system was introduced in 2011
Coins bought at the Dosirak Cafe buy portions from stalls along the arcade
w: An old colonial-era arcade off Seochon's lanes that hit on a clever trick in 2011: swap your won for brass yeopjeon coins, then wander the stalls paying a coin or two for a scoop of this, a fritter of that, until your tray fills. Take it upstairs to eat. A market that turned lunch into a treasure hunt.

hook: A concrete megastructure plugged into central Seoul like a power strip
facts: Built 1966-72 to a design by architect Kim Swoo-geun
Sits on a colonial firebreak strip cleared in 1945, later a postwar shantytown
Korea's first large mixed residential-and-commercial complex
Became the city's electronics-parts mecca through the 1970s and 80s
w: Kim Swoo-geun's half-mile of raw concrete, laid down 1966-72 along a firebreak the colonial regime gouged through the city in 1945. Critics called it a blight; tinkerers called it heaven, packing its arcades with electronics parts you could find nowhere else. From above it resembles a power strip jammed into Seoul. Utopia, half-corroded.

hook: A corten-steel building devoted entirely to locks, latches and keys
facts: Opened in 2004 by Choi Hong-kyu, who made his money in door hardware
Displays around 400 pieces drawn from a 4,000-strong collection
Housed in a rust-skinned weathering-steel building near Naksan
Exhibits range from Korean lockwork to an old chastity belt
w: A man who spent his life selling locks and latches did the obvious thing and built a museum for them, sheathed in rusting steel below Naksan. Inside: 400-odd of his 4,000 pieces, from intricate Korean tumbler-work to keys by the hundred laid out in a glass case, plus one grim chastity belt. A shrine to keeping people out.

hook: A basement bar entered by pulling the right book off a wall of shelves
facts: Reached by clicking the book marked Le Chamber on a hidden bookshelf door
Run by champion bartenders Eom Do-hwan and Lim Jae-jin
Menus arrive as hardcover books listing some 200 whiskies
A regular on the Asia's 50 Best Bars list
w: Down the stairs a wall of books blocks the way until you find the spine marked Le Chamber and pull; the shelf swings open on chandeliers, leather and a piano. Two world-champion bartenders run it, handing you a menu bound as a hardback listing 200 whiskies. The bookshelf gag should be tired by now. Somehow it isn't.

hook: A cocktail den hidden behind a fridge door in a fake fruit shop
facts: Entered through a refrigerator door inside a near-empty fruit stall
Sits on a side street in a residential pocket near Dongdaemun
Tables are marked with a piece of fruit rather than a number
Orders are placed by lifting a payphone to call the bar below
w: On a quiet lane near Dongdaemun sits a fruit shop selling almost nothing, just music and a humming fridge. Open the fridge door and step through into a bar where tables go by fruit instead of numbers and you order by picking up a payphone to ring downstairs. The drinks come theatrically smoked. A grocer's that grew a secret.

hook: A Wonderland-themed bar reached through the back of a flower shop
facts: Found by following white-rabbit signs down stairs to a florist
A secret door at the back of the flower shop opens to the bar
Wood-panelled rooms run on an Alice in Wonderland theme
On Asia's 50 Best Bars lists since 2016
w: Follow the white-rabbit signs down into what reads as an ordinary florist, then watch the staff usher you through a door behind the blooms. Beyond it, wood-panelled rooms run hard on the Wonderland conceit: cotton-candy rims, smoke in skulls, a martini boxed like a gift. Twee on paper, but it commits. Down the rabbit hole, in heels.
